title: practical english usage - Part 48
content:
We   do not use the present perfect in story-telling.

My parents got married in Canada.

- Anna and Daniel have got married! (news) We studied enough to pass the exam. (The exam is over.) - We've studied enough to pass the exam. (The exam is still to come.) Italian.) not Shakespeare probably learnt Italian, (     Shakesjfeare-has-ftrolNtMy-letwfH - My sister has learnt French. (She can speak French now.) present. Compare: together. We   do not use the present perfect if we  are not thinking about the We   use the present perfect if we  are thinking about the past and present 1  not   thinking      about     the   present 48 pr esen   t  pe  rfect    or  past?   ____________ Section 5 Past   and   Perfect     Tenses grammar •  48  present perfect or past?

closer trade links between the two countries.

meeting they discussed the economic situation, and agreed on the need for The Prime Minister has had talks with President Kumani. During a three-hour explosion as the aircraft was taking off,...

There has been a plane crash near Bristol. Witnesses say that there was an Joe has passed his exam! He got 87%.

But when we  give more details, we  usually change to a past tense.

Have you heard? Andy has won a big prize!

We   normally use the present perfect to announce news.

4 news    and   details My   grandfather did a lot for me. (. .. when he was alive) Did you see 'Romeo and Juliet'? (ɪt was on TV  last night.) particular finished time, even if we  do not mention it.

On  the other hand, we  do not use the present perfect when we  are thinking of a You've done a lot for me. (. .. up to now) seen the present production?) or Have you seen 'Romeo and Juliet'? (= Have you ever seen it?     Have you even if we  do not mention it.

We   use the present perfect when we are thinking of a period of 'time up to now', 3 time    not   mentioned For tenses with just and just now, ►    503.

I've seen this film before.

Have you ever been to Chicago?

yet, recently, already), we  normally use the present perfect (►  47.4).

But with words that mean 'at some/any time up to now' (like ever, before, never, not Sam moved three years ago. (     Sam-has-moved three years ago.) - All my friends have moved to London.

When did the accident happen? (     When has-the-aeeident-happened?) - My brother has had an accident. He's in hospital.

What did you do then? (     What haveyou-dene-then?) - What have you done with the car keys? I can't find them.

Tom hurt his leg last week, (     Tom has hurt his-leg-last-week?) - Tom has hurt his leg; he can't walk.

I saw Lucy yesterday, (     /  have seen Lucy yesterday.) - Have you seen Lucy anywhere?

these focus on the past, so they contradict each other. Compare: 1970. This is because the present perfect focuses on the present, and words like finished period of time, like yesterday, last week, then, when, three years ago, in We   do not often use the present perfect with words that refer to a completely 2 finished-time         words:    present      perfect      not   used grammar •  49  present perfect or past: advanced points not The Chinese invented paper. (     The Chinese have invented paper.)    -> Some people think that 'Pericles' was not written by Shakespeare.

That's a nice picture. Did you paint it yourself?

How did you get that bruise?

I'm glad you were born.

'Why are you crying?' 'Granny hit me. (     .. .Granny has hit me.)' Other examples: Who spilt all that coffee on the carpet?

- Somebody has spilt coffee on the carpet.

Who gave you that? (thinking about the past action of giving) - Look what Jack's given me! (thinking about the gift) the past cause, not the present result). Compare: circumstances responsible for a present situation (because we  are thinking about However, we  usually prefer a past tense when we  identify the person, thing or I can't come to your party because I've broken my leg.

together with their present results (►   47.3).

We normally use the present perfect when we  are thinking about past events 1  causes     and   origins:    Who      gave      you     that?
